title: 03 — God is Father
html:
Big Question

How do you imagine God receives you when you return to Him?

When Jesus wanted to explain who God is, he didn't give a theology lecture. He didn't write a treatise on the divine attributes. He didn't draw a diagram. He told a story. The story of a father who couldn't stop loving his son.

He saw him from afar. Even when the son was nothing but a dusty figure on the horizon, the father already recognized him. He didn't wait sitting down. He didn't cross his arms. He didn't say, «Let him come and beg for forgiveness on his knees.» He ran. An elderly man running down the road, his tunic lifted, caring nothing about what others would say, caring nothing about shame. In first-century Middle Eastern culture, a patriarch simply did not run. Running was beneath his dignity. But this father runs. Because love knows nothing of wounded dignity.

And when he reached him, he didn't ask for explanations. He didn't demand a speech of repentance. He didn't even let him finish the rehearsed sentence. He embraced him. He embraced him and covered him with kisses. And then—most scandalous of all—he threw a party. He ordered the fattened calf killed, the one reserved for great occasions. He put a ring on his finger, sandals on his feet, the best robe over his shoulders. Each of those gestures said the same thing: «This is my son. Not a servant. Not a stranger. My son.»

That is the image Jesus gives us of God. Not a judge waiting to punish. Not an accountant adding up debts. Not a watchman lurking for our falls. A Father who runs toward us before we finish turning back. When Jesus wanted to explain God, he didn't give a theology lecture: he told the story of a daddy who couldn't stop loving his child.

The Catechism of the Catholic Church devotes over two hundred paragraphs to unfolding who God the Father is (CCC 198-421). And it does so because this is not one truth among others: it is the truth that changes every other truth. If God is Father, everything looks different. Prayer. Sin. Suffering. Death. Daily life. Everything.

Activity

Imagine you are very, very thirsty. You drink water, but soon you're thirsty again. You drink juice, but it doesn't quite quench you either. That's the human heart: it thirsts for a love that never runs out. Read Luke 15:20-24 aloud. Count how many actions of the father you can find (running, embracing, kissing, giving the ring, putting on sandals, clothing, killing the fattened calf, throwing a party...). Now ask yourself: what does the sheer number of the father's actions tell you about what God is like with me?

1. God's Name: «I Am»

At the burning bush, Moses asks God a question that every human being carries in their heart: «What is your name?» (Ex 3:13). He wants to know who this God is who is calling him. And God answers with something mysterious and profound: «I am who I am» — YHWH (Ex 3:14). This name is not a label; it is a presence. God does not say «my name is such-and-such»; he says «I am here, I am.» He is the God who exists by himself, who depends on nothing and no one, who is the source of everything that is (CCC 205-206).

The name YHWH was so holy for the people of Israel that it was never pronounced. Instead, they said «Lord» (Adonai). This reverence reminds us that God is infinite mystery. But a mystery that draws near, that reveals himself, that wants to be known (CCC 206-207). And this God—Being itself, the Totally Other, the Unreachable—is the very one who reveals himself as Father.

The «I Am» of the burning bush is the «Abba» of Jesus. There are not two gods: a severe one in the Old Testament and a loving one in the New. It is the same God, revealed progressively. What Moses barely glimpsed among flames, we see clearly in the face of Christ (CCC 205-213).

«God said to Moses, 'I AM WHO I AM.' And he said, 'Say this to the people of Israel: I AM has sent me to you.'» — Exodus 3:13-14

2. God the Father in the Old Testament

The revelation of God as Father does not begin with Jesus. Already in the Old Testament, we find glimpses of this truth. Moses reminds Israel: «Is he not your father, who created you?» (Dt 32:6). The prophet Isaiah cries out: «You, Lord, are our father» (Is 63:16). And Psalm 103 describes the heart of God with stunning tenderness: «As a father has compassion on his children, so the Lord has compassion on those who fear him» (Ps 103:13) (CCC 238).

Israel discovered God as father over the course of centuries: in the liberation from Egypt, in the covenant at Sinai, in the exile and return, in God's faithfulness despite the people's infidelities. Little by little, they understood that God is not a distant, cold being, but someone who loves with a father's heart: who corrects when necessary, who forgives without measure, who never abandons (CCC 239).

But there is more. Scripture also uses maternal images to speak of God. Isaiah records this divine promise: «Can a woman forget her nursing child, or show no compassion for the child of her womb? Even if she might forget, yet I will not forget you» (Is 49:15). God is neither male nor female (CCC 239), but his love embraces and surpasses everything good in the love of a father and a mother. Divine fatherhood is the source of every human fatherhood and motherhood (Eph 3:14-15).

The prophet Hosea gives us another window into the Father's heart, describing God's relationship with Israel as that of a parent teaching a toddler to walk:

«When Israel was a child, I loved him, and out of Egypt I called my son... It was I who taught Ephraim to walk, I took them up in my arms... I led them with cords of human kindness, with bands of love. I was to them like those who lift infants to their cheeks. I bent down to them and fed them.» — Hosea 11:1-4

This is not a distant deity. This is a God who bends down to feed his children, who lifts them to his cheek, who teaches them to walk and catches them when they stumble. The Old Testament already knew this God. Jesus would make him fully known.

3. Jesus Reveals the Father: «Abba»

What the Old Testament hinted at, Jesus reveals with a clarity that changes everything. The radical novelty of Jesus is not that God is Father—that was already known—but how he is Father. Jesus uses a word that no one would have dared to use in addressing God: Abba. It is the word a small child uses to speak to his daddy. Not «Almighty Lord,» not «Creator of the universe,» not «Supreme Judge.» It is «Daddy.» «Abba, Father,» Jesus says in Gethsemane (Mk 14:36) (CCC 240-241).

The word «Abba» was scandalous in Jesus' time. No pious Jew would have addressed God with such familiarity. It was too intimate, too close, almost irreverent. But Jesus not only uses it: he teaches us to use it too. «When you pray, say: Father...» (Lk 11:2). He invites us into his own relationship with the Father, to treat God as he treats him.

Jesus does not merely call God Father; he reveals that God is Father in his deepest being. «No one knows the Father except the Son and anyone to whom the Son chooses to reveal him» (Mt 11:27). And he adds: «Whoever has seen me has seen the Father» (Jn 14:9). Jesus is the visible face of the invisible Father. Everything we know about God the Father, we know through Jesus. If you want to know what God is like, look at Jesus: how he welcomes sinners, how he forgives, how he heals, how he weeps, how he gives himself away (CCC 240).

«All things have been handed over to me by my Father; and no one knows the Son except the Father, and no one knows the Father except the Son and anyone to whom the Son chooses to reveal him.» — Matthew 11:27

Connection

Jesus teaches us the Our Father (Mt 6:9-13) as the model of all Christian prayer. Notice: the first word is «Father.» Not «God,» not «Lord,» not «Almighty.» Before asking for anything, before praising, the first thing is to acknowledge the relationship: you are my Father, I am your child. That trust is the foundation of everything else. How would your way of praying change if the first word you say reminded you that you're talking to your Dad?

4. God is Love

St. John says it with a clarity that leaves no room for doubt: «God is love» (1 Jn 4:8,16). He does not say that God has love, as someone who possesses a quality among others. He says that God is love. Love is not something God does; it is what God is. His essence, his deepest identity, is love. That is why he can be Father: because love, by its very nature, gives itself away, pours itself out, generates life (CCC 221-222).

This truth changes everything. If God is love, then:

  • His power is not domination but service and creation.
  • His justice is not vengeance but restoration and healing.
  • His law is not oppression but a path of life and freedom.
  • His correction is not resentment but medicine that cures.

God's love is not like human love, which sometimes fails, grows weary, sets conditions. It is a love that is gratuitous, unconditional, eternal. He doesn't love us because we are good; he loves us because he is good. He doesn't love us when we behave and stop loving us when we fall. His love is constant, like the sun that rises every morning without asking whether we deserve it (CCC 218-221).

St. John also tells us something breathtaking: «See what love the Father has given us, that we should be called children of God; and so we are» (1 Jn 3:1). Not metaphorically. Not wishfully. We really are God's children. Not by nature—only Jesus is Son by nature—but by grace, by adoption. And that changes who we are at the deepest level.

«See what love the Father has given us, that we should be called children of God; and so we are. The reason the world does not know us is that it did not know him.» — 1 John 3:1

Going Deeper

The statement «God is Love» (1 Jn 4:8) has deep Trinitarian roots. The Father loves the Son from all eternity, and that mutual love is the Holy Spirit. God is love not because he loves his creatures (that is a consequence), but because in his innermost being he is a communion of love: three Persons who love each other eternally. St. Augustine explains it this way: the Father is the Lover, the Son is the Beloved, and the Holy Spirit is the Love itself between them. Creation is the free decision to share that love outward (CCC 221-222). Joseph Ratzinger develops this beautifully in Introduction to Christianity: the Christian God is not a solitary monad but a family of love. That is why he can be Father: because love generates relationship. The God we worship is not alone. He has never been alone. He is, in his very essence, a communion of persons—a divine family into which we are invited.

5. The Parable of the Prodigal Son: Portrait of the Father

Jesus told this story in response to those who criticized him for welcoming sinners (Lk 15:1-2). And it is perhaps the most perfect image of God we have. The parable has three characters: the younger son who leaves, the older son who stays, and the father who loves them both. But the true protagonist is the father (CCC 239).

Let's pause on each gesture:

  • He divides the inheritance without reproach. The son has effectively told him: «I wish you were dead; give me your money now.» And the father gives it. He doesn't hold him by force. He loves so much that he respects freedom to its ultimate consequences.
  • He waits every day, scanning the horizon. He hasn't forgotten. He hasn't moved on. He hasn't told himself, «He'll come back when the money runs out.» Every evening his eyes search the road.
  • He runs. This is the most astonishing detail. An Eastern patriarch does not run. It would be degrading. But love knows nothing of offended dignity. The father exposes his reputation for a son who had despised him.
  • He embraces and kisses him before he speaks. He doesn't wait for explanations, he doesn't set conditions. The son comes with a prepared speech («treat me as one of your hired servants»), and the father doesn't let him finish it.
  • He restores his dignity: the best robe (no more rags), the ring (authority of a son), the sandals (slaves went barefoot; sons did not).
  • He throws a feast. The father's joy is overflowing. No reproach, no prior penance, no probationary period. Only embrace and celebration.
  • He also goes out to meet the older son. The one who stayed is also far away. He is bitter, resentful; he believes he has been a slave («I have served you») and not a son. And the father says the most beautiful line in the whole parable: «Son, you are always with me, and all that is mine is yours» (Lk 15:31).

The father in the parable is neither permissive (he lets the son go but doesn't approve his decision) nor absent (he watches every day). He is a father who loves with a freedom that unsettles us. This is the definitive image Jesus gives us of God (CCC 240).

«While he was still far off, his father saw him and was filled with compassion; he ran and put his arms around him and kissed him.» — Luke 15:20

6. Children, Not Slaves: Filial Trust

St. Paul states it with a power that splits history: «You did not receive a spirit of slavery to fall back into fear, but you received a spirit of adoption, through which we cry, 'Abba, Father!'» (Rom 8:15). The Christian faith is not the religion of fear. We are not before a God who watches us to punish us. We stand before a Father who has made us his children by grace, out of pure love (CCC 241, 2782).

This is filial adoption: we are not children of God by nature (only Jesus is), but by grace. God adopts us. He makes us sharers in his divine life. We are not distant creatures trying to earn the favor of a master; we are children who live in the Father's house (CCC 257, 1265-1266).

And in Galatians, Paul adds: «Because you are children, God has sent the Spirit of his Son into our hearts, crying, 'Abba! Father!'» (Gal 4:6). It is the Holy Spirit himself who prays within us, who cries out «Abba» from the depths of our being. We don't even know how to call God «Father» by our own strength—it is the Spirit who enables us, who places that word on our lips.

«For you did not receive a spirit of slavery to fall back into fear, but you received a spirit of adoption, through which we cry, 'Abba, Father!' The Spirit himself bears witness with our spirit that we are children of God.» — Romans 8:15-16

Narrative: St. Thérèse of Lisieux

St. Thérèse of Lisieux (1873-1897) is a Doctor of the Church, and her spirituality is a song to filial trust. As a young Carmelite, she struggled intensely with a question that troubles many souls: How can God be both just and merciful? If God is just, doesn't he have to punish? If he is merciful, isn't he letting things slide?

The breakthrough came when she understood that for God, justice IS mercy. A human judge is impartial because he doesn't know the accused personally. But a father judges his child differently—not by lowering the standard, but by knowing exactly why the child failed, understanding the weakness, and responding with the medicine that heals rather than the punishment that crushes. «God's justice,» she wrote, «is clothed in love.» He knows our weakness as a father knows his child. He doesn't pretend the sin didn't happen; he heals it.

This discovery became the core of her «Little Way.» She called God «Papa» and said: «What pleases God in my little soul is to see me love my littleness and my poverty, it is the blind hope I have in his mercy.» She didn't need great penances or mystical visions. She simply trusted like a child in her father's arms. «I am too small to climb the rough stairway of perfection,» she said. «So I will stay at the bottom and let Jesus carry me.»

That is filial trust lived to its ultimate consequences (CCC 2800-2802). Not earning. Not performing. Just being held.

Filial trust doesn't mean everything will be easy or that there will be no suffering. It means that, in the midst of everything, we know who holds us. A child trusts her father not because everything goes well, but because she knows she is loved. That is the trust Jesus invites us into: «Do not be afraid, little flock, for it is your Father's good pleasure to give you the kingdom» (Lk 12:32) (CCC 2734).

7. The Our Father: Jesus' Gift

When the disciples asked Jesus to teach them to pray, he didn't give them a technique. He gave them words—his words. And those words begin with the most revolutionary address in the history of religion: «Our Father.»

Let this sink in: Jesus, the eternal Son of the Father, invites us to say our Father. Not his Father and our God. Our Father—together, in the same breath, Jesus and us. He shares his own relationship with the Father with us. He doesn't hold it for himself as a privilege. He gives it away. That is why the Our Father is the most perfect prayer: it is the prayer of Jesus himself, placed on our lips (CCC 2759-2766, 2779-2785).

The Catechism notes that «we can invoke God as 'Father' because he is revealed to us by his Son become man and because his Spirit makes him known to us» (CCC 2780). The word «Our» is also crucial: it recognizes that this Father is not merely mine in a private way. He is the Father of all. Every human being who prays the Our Father acknowledges that God is Father of every person they meet. That recognition transforms how we see others. There are no strangers in the family of God (CCC 2792-2793).

«Pray then in this way: Our Father in heaven, hallowed be your name. Your kingdom come. Your will be done, on earth as it is in heaven. Give us this day our daily bread. And forgive us our debts, as we also have forgiven our debtors. And do not bring us to the time of trial, but rescue us from the evil one.» — Matthew 6:9-13

8. God the Father Almighty

The Creed professes: «I believe in God, the Father almighty, Creator of heaven and earth.» Each word carries weight:

  • Father: because he loves us with a father's heart, not as a distant, impersonal being. Relationship comes first.
  • Almighty: but not with a power that crushes, but with a power that creates, saves, rescues. God's omnipotence is not tyranny; it is the force of his love that nothing can stop. God is almighty because he can do whatever he wills... and what he wills is our good (CCC 268-271).
  • Creator: because everything that exists comes from his gratuitous love. He didn't need to create; he created out of love, to share his life and his joy (CCC 295-296).

God is also Truth: «God is light and in him there is no darkness at all» (1 Jn 1:5). He cannot deceive or be deceived. His Word is faithful; his promises are fulfilled. We can trust him completely because he is Truth itself. He will never fail us. He will never break a promise. What he says, he does (CCC 214-217).

9. Providence: The Father Who Never Abandons

«I am who I am» also means «I am always here.» God does not create the world and then leave it running like a watchmaker abandoning his watch. He sustains it in every instant. Divine Providence is God's continuous action that preserves, accompanies, and governs all creation with wisdom and love (CCC 301-308).

Jesus asks us to trust in this Providence the way children trust their father:

«Do not worry about your life, what you will eat or what you will drink, or about your body, what you will wear... Look at the birds of the air: they neither sow nor reap nor gather into barns, and yet your heavenly Father feeds them... Consider the lilies of the field, how they grow: they neither toil nor spin... But if God so clothes the grass of the field, which is alive today and tomorrow is thrown into the oven, will he not much more clothe you—you of little faith?» — Matthew 6:25-30

Providence doesn't eliminate problems or spare us from work. But it gives us a certainty: we are not alone. There is a Father who knows what we need before we ask him (Mt 6:8) and who works all things for our good (Rom 8:28). Living from Providence doesn't mean being passive; it means working with the peace of knowing that the final outcome is in the hands of a Father who loves us.

10. The Problem of Evil: A Father Who Allows Suffering

We arrive at the hardest question of faith: «If God is a loving and almighty Father, why does he allow suffering, especially the suffering of the innocent?» The Catechism doesn't offer an easy answer—because there isn't one—but it gives keys that illuminate the mystery (CCC 309-314).

  • God does not want evil. Evil entered the world through sin, through the misuse of human freedom (CCC 311).
  • God respects our freedom. He could prevent every evil, but he would have to annul our freedom, and then we would stop being human and become puppets (CCC 311).
  • God can bring good out of evil. He didn't «want» evil, but he permits it because he can transform it into the occasion for a greater good. Like a composer who, faced with a wrong note, writes a more beautiful melody (CCC 312).
  • The Cross is the definitive answer. God doesn't give us explanations from above; he comes down, becomes one of us, suffers with us and for us. On the Cross, God takes on the evil of the world and transforms it into salvation. The worst crime in history became the greatest source of life (CCC 313).

St. John Paul II, who suffered greatly—lost his mother at age nine, his brother, his father, lived under Nazism and communism, was shot by a terrorist—wrote: «Suffering is present in the world to unleash love, to give birth to works of love toward neighbor, to transform all human civilization into the civilization of love.» God doesn't magically eliminate suffering; he takes it on and gives us the strength to go through it.

11. Wounds of Fatherhood and Healing in God

There is a difficult but necessary topic: many people struggle to call God «Father» because their experience of a human father was painful. Absence, abandonment, abuse, indifference, impossible demands, favoritism. The word «father» evokes wounds. And when the Church says «God is Father,» some souls close up in legitimate self-defense.

The Catechism is sensitive to this. It clearly states that God is neither male nor female (CCC 239), that his love surpasses every human category, that the maternal images of God in Scripture remind us that all human fatherhood and motherhood are only pale reflections of divine love. God's fatherhood is not like human fatherhood magnified; it is human fatherhood that is an imperfect image of divine fatherhood (Eph 3:14-15).

This means:

  • God is not like your human father if your father failed you.
  • God heals the wounds your human father caused.
  • God is the measure of every father; human fathers are imperfect approximations.

If you find it hard to call God «Father,» you can start with «Abba» (a new word, free from the baggage of your language), or lean on the maternal images of Scripture. God's love is bigger than your wounds, and precisely in his fatherhood you can find healing for what human sin broke.

God is Father of ALL, not just Christians. The fatherhood of God extends to every human being without exception. Every person was created by him, is sustained by him, is loved by him. Christ died for all. The Holy Spirit is offered to all. There is no one who is not a child of God by creation, and there is no one whom God does not desire to make his child by grace. The Church exists to announce this truth to every human being: You have a Father. You are not an orphan. You are not an accident. You are loved (CCC 27-30, 2792-2793).

For Reflection

1. What strikes you most about the image of the father in the Parable of the Prodigal Son: that he ran, that he embraced his son without letting him finish, that he threw a party? Why?

Take a moment to imagine the scene. Not as a beautiful painting, but as something that is happening to you, right now.

2. If God is «Abba»—Daddy—what changes in your way of praying? Do you pray more like a slave asking permission or a child who trusts?

Many of us carry an image of God as a severe judge, someone waiting for us to fail. But Jesus didn't present God that way. Where does your image of God come from?

3. The parable has two sons. Which one do you identify with more today: the one who left and needs to return, or the one who stayed but is resentful? Why?

The older son is the harder one to recognize. He believes he has been a slave («I have served you»), not a son. Have you treated God like a boss you owe favors to?

4. Have you struggled to see God as Father because of painful experiences with human father figures?

If so, you are not alone. God's fatherhood is not a magnified copy of human fatherhood; human fatherhood is an imperfect copy of the divine. Can you ask God to heal that wound?

5. What would you do differently this week if you truly believed that God is a Father who runs toward you and can bring good out of every evil?

Name one concrete situation in your life where this trust would change your decisions.

Key Idea

God is not a judge waiting for your failure to punish you; he is a Father who runs toward you before you finish turning back, who embraces you without asking for explanations, and who throws a party when you come home.

Cultural Treasures

Art Rembrandt, «The Return of the Prodigal Son» (1668, Hermitage Museum, St. Petersburg). This painting is one of the most moving works in art history. Rembrandt painted it at the end of his life, when he had lost everything: money, fame, wife, children. Perhaps he recognized himself in that son who returns defeated. Look at the father's hands: they are different. One is masculine (strong, protective) and the other feminine (gentle, welcoming). It is said that Rembrandt wanted to represent the paternal and maternal love of God. The light seems to come from heaven itself and concentrate on the embrace.

Book Henri Nouwen, The Return of the Prodigal Son (1992). Nouwen spent hours contemplating Rembrandt's painting and wrote a meditation that has become a spiritual classic. The book traces the three stages of the spiritual life: being the younger son (coming home), being the older son (learning to rejoice with others), and finally, becoming the father (loving unconditionally). Nouwen discovered in that painting the healing of his own wounds and the deep vocation of every Christian. «The true return,» he wrote, «is to become the father.»

Saint St. Thérèse of Lisieux and her «Little Way». Thérèse (1873-1897) is a Doctor of the Church, and her spirituality is a hymn to filial trust. She called God «Papa» and said: «What pleases God in my little soul is to see me love my littleness and my poverty, it is the blind hope I have in his mercy.» She didn't need great penances or mystical visions. She simply trusted like a little girl in her father's arms. Her Story of a Soul is a book that has transformed millions of lives. It is one of the most accessible and beloved spiritual autobiographies ever written, and it has been translated into every major language. Her discovery that divine justice is really mercy clothed in fatherly love continues to heal souls who fear a distant, angry God.

Art Michelangelo, «The Creation of Adam» (Sistine Chapel, 1511). God the Father extends his hand toward Adam, and their fingers almost touch. That tension, that minimal space between the fingers, is the visual image of what the Catechism teaches: God seeks us, calls us, desires us. The desire is not one-sided. It is a dialogue initiated by the Father and awaited by the child. Look at God the Father's face: it is full of urgency, full of wanting. He is not a passive, distant creator. He is reaching. Always reaching. His arm is fully extended, his body leaning forward, his hair and beard swept back as if by the wind of his own motion. This is a God who moves toward us.

Music «Good, Good Father» by Chris Tomlin (2015). This contemporary worship song has become an anthem in churches around the world. The lyrics capture the heart of the Gospel: «You're a good, good Father, it's who you are... and I'm loved by you, it's who I am.» Sometimes the simplest words carry the deepest truths. This song is a way to enter into the filial trust the Catechism proposes.

Film The Mission (1986). In the final scene, Father Gabriel walks among soldiers carrying the Blessed Sacrament, singing, while guns fire around him. He doesn't meet violence with violence. It is the image of a love that doesn't defend itself because it trusts completely in the Father. It is filial trust taken to its ultimate consequence: «Abba, Father, all things are possible to you; remove this cup from me; yet not what I will, but what you will» (Mk 14:36).
